is a stance in which the boxer has the right hand and the right foot forward, leading with right [jabs](/wiki/Jab "Jab"), and following with a left [cross](/wiki/Cross_(boxing) "Cross (boxing)") right [hook](/wiki/Hook_(boxing) "Hook (boxing)"). It is the normal stance for a [left-handed](/wiki/Left-handed "Left-handed") boxer. The corresponding boxing designation for a right-handed boxer is the [orthodox stance](/wiki/Orthodox_stance "Orthodox stance"), which is generally a mirror-image of the southpaw stance. In [American English](/wiki/American_English "American English"), "southpaw" generally refers to a person who is left-handed. While many advantages have been theorized for left-handedness in sports,[[1]](#cite_note-sorokowski2014-1) many studies have found no impact at the professional level.[[2]](#cite_note-sopiarz2019-2)[[3]](#cite_note-baker2013-3)
Sorokowski, Piotr, Agnieszka Sabiniewicz, and Sławomir Wacewicz. "The influence of the boxing stance on performance in professional boxers." AnthropologicAl review 77.3 (2014): 347-353.
SOPIARZ, JOSH. "Fighters and Fathers: Managing Masculinity in Contemporary Boxing Cinema." THE POPULAR CULTURE STUDIES JOURNAL (2019): 257.
Baker, Joseph, and Jörg Schorer. "The Southpaw advantage?-lateral preference in mixed martial arts." PloS one 8.11 (2013): e79793.

["I read somewhere that all major league baseball stadiums must point in the same direction (3rd base line north). Is this true?"](http://www.infoplease.com/askeds/baseball-stadiums-oriented-sun.html). Info Please.

Wilton, Dave (July 5, 2012), [*Southpaw*](https://web.archive.org/web/20160126123748/http://www.wordorigins.org/index.php/site/comments/southpaw), Word Origins, archived from [the original](http://www.wordorigins.org/index.php/site/comments/southpaw) on January 26, 2016, retrieved January 23, 2016

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Hand-to-hand combat terminology

For other uses, see [Southpaw (disambiguation)](/wiki/Southpaw_(disambiguation) "Southpaw (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/95/AlMcCoy_boxer.jpg)](/wiki/File:AlMcCoy_boxer.jpg)

[Al McCoy](/wiki/Al_McCoy_(boxer) "Al McCoy (boxer)"), world champion in the 1910s, displaying southpaw stance with right hand and right foot to the fore

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/6e/RuslanChagaev.jpg)](/wiki/File:RuslanChagaev.jpg)

[Ruslan Chagaev](/wiki/Ruslan_Chagaev "Ruslan Chagaev") in southpaw stance

In [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") and some other sports, a **southpaw stance** is a stance in which the boxer has the right hand and the right foot forward, leading with right [jabs](/wiki/Jab "Jab"), and following with a left [cross](/wiki/Cross_(boxing) "Cross (boxing)") right [hook](/wiki/Hook_(boxing) "Hook (boxing)"). It is the normal stance for a [left-handed](/wiki/Left-handed "Left-handed") boxer. The corresponding boxing designation for a right-handed boxer is the [orthodox stance](/wiki/Orthodox_stance "Orthodox stance"), which is generally a mirror-image of the southpaw stance. In [American English](/wiki/American_English "American English"), "southpaw" generally refers to a person who is left-handed. While many advantages have been theorized for left-handedness in sports,[[1]](#cite_note-sorokowski2014-1) many studies have found no impact at the professional level.[[2]](#cite_note-sopiarz2019-2)[[3]](#cite_note-baker2013-3)

## Previous uses of the term southpaw

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southpaw_stance&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Previous uses of the term southpaw")]

The "American Heritage Dictionary of the English Language" cites the conventional wisdom that the word "southpaw" originated "from the practice in baseball of arranging the diamond with the batter facing east to avoid the afternoon sun."[[4]](#cite_note-4) Though many claim that the term originated due to the orientation of baseball playing fields in order to keep the sun out of the players' eyes and the resulting alignment of a left-handed pitcher's throwing arm causing the pitcher to have his left hand on the south side of his body,[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) the term had been used decades prior to that to indicate "not-usual".[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southpaw_stance&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: See also")]

* [List of southpaw stance boxers](/wiki/List_of_southpaw_stance_boxers "List of southpaw stance boxers")
* [Footedness](/wiki/Footedness "Footedness")

2. **[^](#cite_ref-sopiarz2019_2-0)** SOPIARZ, JOSH. "Fighters and Fathers: Managing Masculinity in Contemporary Boxing Cinema." THE POPULAR CULTURE STUDIES JOURNAL (2019): 257.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-baker2013_3-0)** Baker, Joseph, and Jörg Schorer. "The Southpaw advantage?-lateral preference in mixed martial arts." PloS one 8.11 (2013): e79793.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** [*Why are left-handers called "southpaws"?*](https://www.history.com/news/why-are-left-handers-called-southpaws), History.com, Aug 12, 2015
